content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
September 11, 1997
The Honorable Ross Johnson
California State Senate
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-97-456
Dear Senator Johnson:
This letter responds to your request for informal assistance
1 abbut the Political Reform
Act (the “Act”).
2
QUESTIONS
1. What are the record keeping obligations of a candidate in terms of ascribing the
contribution to the primary or general election? Must the funds be deposited in segregated
accounts?
2. When may funds ascribed to a general election campaign be expended?
3. In January 1998, for example, a person contributes $200 to a candidate for the State
Senate in 1998. Must the contributor indicate ifthe contribution is intended for the primary or
general election? Or is the candidate free to unilaterally make that decision on behalf ofthe
contributor to decide how the contribution should be ascribed?
4. A candidate has won the 1998 primary election and will be on the 1998 general
election ballot in November. That candidate has a debt from the primary. In August 1998, for
example, may an individual, who has previously not contributed to a candidate, contribute
$1,000 to that candidate, designating $500 for the primary and $500 for the general?
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c)(3).)
2 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-456
Page 2
5. If a candidate has raised general election campaign funds prior to the primary and then
loses the primary, what rules apply to those election funds? Do they immediately become
surplus funds? What if the candidate runs as a write-in in the general election?
RESPONSE
Your questions address subjects currently under active consideration by the Commission.
The Commission considered emergency regulations which covered most of the questions in your
advice request at the July 1997 meeting; however, ultimately, the Commission did not adopt the
emergency regulations at that meeting. After expressing some initial policy inclinations, the
Commissioners instead instructed the staff to proceed under the normal rulemaking process. The
first step in that process was the interested persons’ meeting which you attended in August 1997.
The staff is now refining the draft regulations originally presented as proposed emergency
regulations in July. As of now, the Commission is scheduled to consider these matters again at
the November 1997 meeting.
Given the degree to which the Commissioners themselves are already engaged in the
resolution ofthese issues, we respectfully decline to advise pending the Commission’s further
actions. Unlike other situations in which staff advice has preceded Commission action, the
Commissioners have already indicated tentative views on these issues. Under these
circumstances, we must decline to respond to your question. We apologize for any
inconvenience this delay may precipitate.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
eneral Counsel
I
ffd
y:
John yergelh
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
